name: OpenBindings Binding Invoker
description: Interface for invoking bindings in specific formats across a service boundary. A binding invoker interprets a binding format (e.g., OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, gRPC, MCP) and knows how to invoke operations through it. This contract is the wire form of binding invocation: it governs remote invokers (delegates, hosted invokers, anything reached over a transport). In-process implementations (the SDKs' invocation handles) realize the same semantics natively and are not required to expose frames; the frame protocol is the handle, serialized. invokeBinding is a typed bidirectional I/O operation: the caller streams BindingInvokerInputFrame messages in (starting with an `open` frame that carries the BindingInvocationInput, followed by zero or more `input` frames and a terminating `close`), and the service streams BindingInvokerOutputFrame messages back (zero or more `output` frames plus a single terminal `complete` or `error` frame). The same shape covers unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional bindings; cardinality is observed by how the caller drives the frames, not declared in the contract. Authentication and other runtime prerequisites are not carried in the OBI document; instead, when a binding cannot proceed because required context is missing, invokeBinding fails with a CONTEXT_REQUIRED error enumerating what must be satisfied, and the runtime resolves it into context and retries. This keeps prerequisites out of the static interface and uniform across every binding format.

FormatInfo.description: Information about a supported binding format.

location.description: Location of the binding source (path or URL).

BindingContext.description: Opaque runtime context for binding invocation, typically supplied by the runtime from its store. Well-known credential fields: 'bearerToken' (string), 'apiKey' (string), 'basic' (object with 'username' and 'password'), 'accessToken' / 'refreshToken' / 'expiresAt' for OAuth. Well-known transport fields: 'headers' / 'cookies' / 'environment' (objects with string values) and 'metadata' (object with arbitrary values). Implementations and callers MAY add additional fields for session state, consent flags, or any other invoker-managed runtime data; drivers SHOULD ignore fields they don't recognize.

ContextRequirement.description: One prerequisite that must be satisfied before a binding can be invoked, resolved into context. `type` names the requirement family; the remaining fields are type-specific. The well-known `auth.*` family resolves into BindingContext credential fields: `auth.bearer` produces 'bearerToken', `auth.apiKey` produces 'apiKey', `auth.basic` produces 'basic', and `auth.oauth2` produces 'accessToken' (carrying flow fields such as 'authorizeUrl', 'tokenUrl', and 'scopes'). Other families (e.g. 'approval.user', 'config.value', 'account.link') resolve into their own context fields. A runtime dispatches each requirement to a resolver registered for its `type`.

type.description: Requirement type identifier, e.g. 'auth.bearer', 'auth.oauth2', 'auth.basic', 'auth.apiKey', 'approval.user', 'config.value'. Resolvers register against these; an unrecognized type is unsatisfiable by a runtime that has no resolver for it.

description.description: Human-readable description shown to a user or operator during resolution (e.g. an approval prompt message).

durable.description: Whether resolved context for this requirement MAY be cached under the challenge `key` and reused for later invocations (true; e.g. credentials), or MUST be satisfied freshly for every invocation and MUST NOT be cached (false; e.g. a one-shot user approval). Defaults to true. A requirement family typically fixes this value.

ContextRequiredDetails.description: The `details` payload of an InvocationError whose `code` is CONTEXT_REQUIRED, and the return shape of prepareBinding. Enumerates the runtime context the invoker needs before it can proceed. `alternatives` are disjunctive: satisfying ANY one suffices. The `requirements` within a single alternative are conjunctive: ALL must be satisfied. This OR-of-AND shape captures real authentication semantics such as 'OAuth2 OR (apiKey AND clientCert)', which a flat list cannot.

target.description: The target the binding addresses — typically its endpoint URL or host. The runtime uses this to locate and store any context it resolves; how it derives a storage key from the target (for example, normalizing to host[:port] so formats sharing a host share context) is the runtime's policy, not part of this contract. The invoker reports the target and neither keys nor stores.

alternatives.description: Disjunctive set of ways to satisfy the requirement. The runtime selects one alternative whose every requirement it can resolve.

requirements.description: Conjunctive set: every requirement in this alternative must be resolved for the alternative to be satisfied.

InvocationError.description: Structured error carried by an `error` output frame. Indicates terminal failure of the invocation.

code.description: Machine-readable error code (e.g., ERR_CANCELLED, ERR_VALIDATION_FAILED, ERR_PROTOCOL, CONTEXT_REQUIRED).

details.description: Optional structured details specific to the error code. For code CONTEXT_REQUIRED, `details` is a ContextRequiredDetails object.

BindingInvocationInput.description: Initial payload carried by the `open` input frame: identifies the binding to invoke and provides context.

BindingInvokerInputFrame.description: One frame from the caller into invokeBinding. The caller streams a sequence: exactly one `open` frame first (carrying the BindingInvocationInput), then zero or more `input` frames carrying values to deliver to the underlying binding, then exactly one `close` frame signaling no more input.

oneOf.title: open
oneOf.description: Open the invocation. MUST be the first frame on the input stream.

oneOf.title: input
oneOf.description: Write one input value to the underlying binding. The value's schema is determined by the underlying binding's operation, not by this contract.

value.description: One input value, opaque at this layer.

oneOf.title: close
oneOf.description: Caller signals that no more input frames will be sent. Idempotent.

BindingInvokerOutputFrame.description: One frame from the service back to the caller. The service streams a sequence: zero or more non-terminal frames (`output`, `input_closed`) followed by exactly one terminal frame (`complete` or `error`).

oneOf.title: output
oneOf.description: One output value from the underlying binding. The value's schema is determined by the underlying binding's operation, not by this contract.

value.description: One output value, opaque at this layer.

oneOf.title: input_closed
oneOf.description: Binding has closed the input side from its end (e.g., a unary binding closes input after the first read). Subsequent caller `input` frames are ignored (never written; see rule 3). The invocation continues; outputs may still flow.

oneOf.title: complete
oneOf.description: Terminal: the underlying binding completed normally. MUST be the last frame.

oneOf.title: error
oneOf.description: Terminal: the underlying binding failed. MUST be the last frame. Carries a structured InvocationError.

openbindings.binding-invoker.listFormats.description: List binding formats this invoker supports.

openbindings.binding-invoker.invokeBindi.description:
Invoke a resolved binding via a typed I/O frame stream. The caller writes a sequence of BindingInvokerInputFrame messages (starting with an `open` frame); the service emits a sequence of BindingInvokerOutputFrame messages ending in either a `complete` or `error` frame. The same shape covers all cardinalities of the underlying binding (unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, bidirectional).

## Normative conformance rules

Service implementations of this operation MUST conform to the following rules. They describe the wire contract, not any particular transport; in-process implementations realize the same semantics through their SDK's native invocation handle, whose behavior these rules mirror.

1. **First frame.** The first input frame received MUST be an `open` frame. If the first frame is any other kind, the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_PROTOCOL` and process no further input.

2. **Single open.** Exactly one `open` frame is permitted per invocation. If a second `open` frame arrives after the first, the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_PROTOCOL`.

3. **Input after input closure (either side).** Once input is closed from either side — the caller has sent its `close` frame, or the implementation has emitted an `input_closed` output frame — any subsequent `input` frame MUST be ignored: it is not written to the underlying binding, the invocation continues, and outputs still flow. The implementation SHOULD surface a diagnostic for ignored frames. Late frames after `input_closed` are an inherent race (the frame may already be in flight when `input_closed` is emitted) and are never a protocol violation; this mirrors the SDK handles, where a write after input closure rejects non-terminally without affecting the invocation.

4. **Exactly one terminal output frame.** The output stream MUST end with exactly one terminal frame (`complete` or `error`). The implementation MUST NOT emit further output frames after a terminal frame. If both completion and failure conditions arise during processing, the implementation chooses one terminal frame (typically `error`, since errors take precedence over completion) and emits only that one.

5. **Transport closure without a terminal frame.** If the underlying transport closes without a terminal output frame having been emitted, the consumer side MUST synthesize a terminal `error` frame with code `ERR_TRANSPORT_CLOSED` before propagating closure to its caller.

6. **Discriminator semantics.** The `discriminator` keyword in the frame schemas is informative under JSON Schema 2020-12; validators that don't recognize it MUST still validate frames correctly by evaluating `oneOf`. Implementations dispatch by matching the `kind` property's `const` value against the variant.

7. **`additionalProperties: false`.** Frame variants declare `additionalProperties: false`. Implementations MUST reject input frames with unknown properties (using `ERR_PROTOCOL`). Output frames SHOULD likewise reject extra properties on the consumer side as a protocol violation.

8. **Missing context (CONTEXT_REQUIRED).** If the binding cannot be invoked because required runtime context is missing (credentials, approvals, configuration, and the like), the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `CONTEXT_REQUIRED` whose `details` is a ContextRequiredDetails object, and it MUST do so before emitting any `output` frame and before causing any observable side effect on the target. This pre-execution guarantee is what makes the runtime's resolve-and-retry safe for non-idempotent operations.

9. **Resolve and retry.** On receiving CONTEXT_REQUIRED, a runtime selects one `alternative` whose every `requirement` it can resolve (alternatives are disjunctive; requirements within one alternative are conjunctive), resolves each requirement into context, MAY persist context for requirements whose `durable` is true (the default), under a storage key it derives from `details.target`, for reuse, and MAY retry the invocation with the augmented context. Context for a `durable: false` requirement MUST NOT be persisted or reused and MUST be re-satisfied on each invocation. A runtime SHOULD bound retries; an implementation SHOULD NOT re-challenge for context it was supplied on the current attempt, and SHOULD instead emit a non-CONTEXT_REQUIRED error when supplied context proves insufficient, so the loop terminates.

10. **Requirement types.** `auth.bearer`, `auth.basic`, `auth.apiKey`, and `auth.oauth2` are the first standard requirement family and resolve into the corresponding BindingContext credential fields. Implementations MAY define further families (e.g. `approval.user`, `config.value`). An unrecognized requirement `type` is unsatisfiable by a runtime that lacks a resolver for it; such a requirement simply makes its enclosing alternative unselectable.

openbindings.binding-invoker.prepareBind.description: Preflight: report the context a binding would require before invocation, without invoking it or causing side effects. Returns a ContextRequiredDetails the runtime can resolve proactively (good for UX, e.g. prompting for auth before a user acts), or null when the invoker cannot determine requirements without invoking (some targets only reveal them via a live CONTEXT_REQUIRED challenge). Every service implementation provides this operation; returning null is the conformant answer whenever requirements cannot be determined statically, so the operation is always satisfiable. prepareBinding is advisory: the reactive CONTEXT_REQUIRED error from invokeBinding is the authoritative source of requirements. When `context` is supplied on the input, the result reports only the requirements still unsatisfied by that context.
